architectural_implication: SIC candidate generation → SAE re-ranking → LLM synthesis
summary: T01 (PASS): Year-demeaning increases signal from rho=0.003 to 0.022, confirming the raw global correlation was suppressed by market-regime noise — the underlying company-specific signal is real. T02 (FAIL): Top-1% SAE pairs are only 1.24x enriched for same-SIC vs baseline, meaning SAE's highest-similarity pairs are mostly cross-industry — the inversion is not a SIC-composition artifact but reflects genuine cross-industry noise. T03 (PASS, qualified): SAE nearest-neighbor retrieval beats random (60% hit rate at K=10, lift CI excludes zero), but SAE top-10 mean correlation (0.222) underperforms the SIC baseline (0.244) — SAE has global retrieval signal but is not the best global retriever. T04 (PASS): Within-SIC, SAE re-ranking produces +0.055 lift at K=1 over random same-industry peers, with CI excluding zero across all K values and all 25 years — this is the strongest and most consistent result. T05 (PASS): Graph topology adds +0.098 correlation above magnitude-matched controls, confirming that which companies are nearest neighbors matters beyond raw cosine magnitude. Combined verdict: SAE features carry real retrieval signal, but their comparative advantage is within-industry discrimination, not global similarity. The product architecture should use SIC codes for candidate generation and SAE cosine for re-ranking within industry, not as a standalone retrieval engine.
next_steps: Run 1B factor adjustment to check if T04 within-SIC signal is factor-driven
Run 1C permutation test for Layer 1 completeness
Design multi-signal retrieval pipeline (Layer 3): SIC → SAE re-rank → LLM synthesis
